input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The system under study is a **lab-scale gantry crane**, a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) inspired by harbor cranes in the Port of Antwerp. It is designed to move small pucks (representing containers) between predefined locations. The physical twin (PT) consists of a **Crane Frame**, two **stepper motors** (one for cart movement and one for hoisting), **TMC4671-Eval motor controllers**, an **electromagnet** for gripping pucks, a **swing angle encoder** to measure hoist swinging motion, and an **anemometer** to measure wind speed. The PT is powered by a **PSU (Power Supply Unit)** and controlled via an **Arduino** interfacing with the sensors and actuators. The digital twin (DT) is a software-based replica that includes **CAD models**, a **kinematic model**, and services for optimal control, historical data logging, visualization, and continuous validation. The DT operates in tandem with the PT to improve operational efficiency and enable research on digital twin methodologies.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The physical acting components of the DT constellation include:
  - **Two stepper motors** (cart motor and hoist motor) controlled via **TMC4671-Eval motor controllers** for precise movement.
  - An **electromagnet** at the end of the hoist to pick up and release pucks (containers).
  - An **Arduino-based control system** that interfaces with the motor controllers and sensors to execute commands from the DT or direct operator input. The Arduino also manages the **PSU** for power distribution to the electronics.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The physical sensing components include:
  - A **swing angle encoder** to measure the swinging motion of the hoist, critical for trajectory validation and fault detection.
  - An **anemometer** to measure wind speed, which can affect crane stability and trajectory accuracy.
  - **Motor controllers (TMC4671-Eval)** that provide feedback on motor position and status, enabling closed-loop control.
  - The **Arduino** collects data from these sensors and transmits it to the DT for real-time monitoring and analysis.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The interactions from the physical to the virtual world involve:
  - **Real-time data streaming** from the Arduino to the **Logging Application** via a **Python interface**, capturing sensor data (swing angle, wind speed, motor status) and crane state (position, velocity).
  - **Historical data logging** to a **TimescaleDB** database, optimized for time-series data, storing trajectories, sensor readings, and operational states.
  - **MQTT-based signaling** between the **Physical Crane Interface** (Python) and the **MQTT Broker (Eclipse Mosquitto)** to relay sensor data and control signals to DT services (e.g., Trajectory Generator, Continuous Validation).
  - **Continuous validation** where executed trajectories are compared against simulated trajectories (via the **Simulation Service**) to detect deviations and validate model fidelity.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The virtual-to-physical interaction is mediated through:
  - **Control Application**: A Python application that uses the **Physical Crane Interface** to send control commands (e.g., trajectory setpoints) directly to the **TMC4671-Eval motor controllers** (not the Arduino). The Arduino is not involved in this control loop.
  - **Trajectory Generator Service**: Computes optimal swing-free trajectories using the kinematic model and sends these to the Control Application for execution.
  - **Human Machine Interface (HMI)**: Allows operators to manually configure and control the crane, with real-time feedback from the digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The DT provides the following services:
  - **Optimal Trajectory Generation**: Uses a kinematic model to compute swing-free trajectories for the crane, minimizing residual motion at the end of movement.
  - **Historical Data Logging**: Continuously logs crane state (position, velocity, sensor data) to **TimescaleDB** for post-analysis and trend monitoring.
  - **Data Visualization**: A **Grafana dashboard** visualizes real-time and historical data, including trajectory traces, sensor readings, and validation metrics (e.g., confidence bounds from simulations).
  - **Continuous Validation**: Compares executed trajectories with simulated trajectories using distance metrics (e.g., Euclidean distance) and thresholds. Alerts are generated if deviations exceed acceptable limits.
  - **Simulation Service**: Simulates crane behavior and controller responses for offline testing and validation of trajectories before physical execution.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale:
The digital twin operates in **real-time** with continuous synchronization between the physical and virtual systems. Key interactions include:
  - **Continuous logging** of crane state data (e.g., motor positions, swing angles) to the TimescaleDB database.
  - **Real-time validation** of executed trajectories against simulations, with alerts generated if discrepancies exceed predefined thresholds.
  - **Immediate feedback loops** for control adjustments via the Control Application and HMI. The exact time-scale for synchronization is not specified in the document, but the system is designed for **low-latency operation** given its use of local deployment and MQTT.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities:
The DT system is implemented in a **centralized yet modular architecture**, with the following multiplicities:
  - **Physical Twin (PT)**: A single instance of the gantry crane hardware.
  - **Digital Twin (DT)**: Composed of **six containers** (Database, Dashboard, Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation, MQTT Broker), each running as a separate service but orchestrated as a unified system.
  - **Decentralized services**: Services like the **Trajectory Generator** and **Simulation Service** operate independently but communicate via **MQTT** and shared **TimescaleDB** data.
  - **Scalability**: The architecture supports adding new services (e.g., fault detection, predictive maintenance) without redesigning the core system, as demonstrated in extensions like **DarTwin notation** for evolution.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The DT covers the following lifecycle stages of the PT:
  - **As Designed**: Represented by **CAD models** (Autodesk Fusion 360) and the **kinematic model** used for trajectory planning.
  - **As Manufactured**: Validated through **continuous validation** during operation, comparing simulated (designed) behavior with executed trajectories.
  - **As Operated**: Enables real-time monitoring, fault detection, and adaptive control based on operational data (e.g., wind conditions, wear-and-tear indicators).
  The DT does not explicitly model **maintenance or end-of-life stages**, but its **historical logging** and **fault detection** services could be extended for these phases.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The DT includes the following models and data:
  - **CAD Models**: 3D representations of the crane (Crane Frame, motors, sensors) created in **Autodesk Fusion 360**.
  - **Kinematic Model**: A mathematical model of the crane’s mechanics, used by the **Trajectory Generator Service** to compute optimal paths.
  - **Simulation Model**: A digital replica of the crane and its controllers, implemented in the **Simulation Service** for offline testing.
  - **Time-Series Data**: Logged to **TimescaleDB** (PostgreSQL-based), including sensor readings (swing angle, wind speed), motor commands, and trajectory data.
  - **Validation Data**: Metrics comparing executed vs. simulated trajectories, stored for analysis and threshold tuning.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The DT leverages the following tools and enablers:
  - **Programming**: Python for all DT services (**gantrylib** module), with **pip** for package management.
  - **Containerization**: **Docker** for deploying the **Database (TimescaleDB)**, **MQTT Broker (Eclipse Mosquitto)**, and **Dashboard (Grafana)** via a **docker-compose** file.
  - **Database**: **TimescaleDB** (PostgreSQL extension) for time-series data storage, chosen for SQL compatibility, time-series optimization, and open-source licensing.
  - **Visualization**: **Grafana** for real-time and historical data dashboards, supporting multiple data sources.
  - **Messaging**: **MQTT (Eclipse Mosquitto)** for lightweight, publish-subscribe communication between services.
  - **Hardware Abstraction**: **Arduino** for sensor/actuator interfacing, with custom **KiCad** shield designs for connectivity.
  - **Modeling**: **Capella (Arcadia)** for Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) in teaching contexts.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The DT constellation is organized as follows:
  - **Physical Layer**: The **Physical Crane** (hardware) interacts with the **Physical Crane Interface** (Python) via the **Arduino**.
  - **Digital Layer**: Composed of **six containers** (Database, Dashboard, Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation, MQTT Broker), each with distinct responsibilities but interconnected via **MQTT** and shared data.
  - **Operator Layer**: The **Human Machine Interface (HMI)** provides a front-end for configuration, control, and monitoring.
  - **Researcher Layer**: Enables experimentation with **continuous validation**, **fault detection**, and **DarTwin notation** for system evolution.
  The architecture follows a **C4 Model** for visualization, with clear separation of concerns between physical and digital components.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process involves:
  - **Development**: The DT was developed iteratively, starting with basic logging and visualization, then adding **continuous validation**, **trajectory optimization**, and **fault detection**.
  - **Quality Assurance**: Validated through **publications** (e.g., [4], [5], [6]) and **teaching use cases** (e.g., BIP at University of Antwerp, SpaceRaise doctoral school).
  - **Evolution**: Documented using **DarTwin notation** to capture common evolution patterns (e.g., adding new sensors, extending to harbor systems).
  - **Milestones**:
    - Initial version used for **continuous validation** research ([5]).
    - Extended for **fault detection** using time-series classification ([6]).
    - Expanded in teaching to include **harbor and ship simulations** (BIP 2024-2025).
  - **Upgrades**: Future work includes **custom driver boards** to reduce costs, **physical simulation** for virtual commissioning, and integration with **digital twin frameworks** (e.g., Eclipse Ditto).
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
Fidelity and validity are ensured through:
  - **Continuous Validation**: Executed trajectories are compared to simulated trajectories using **distance metrics** (e.g., Euclidean distance) with configurable thresholds. Deviations trigger alerts via **Grafana**.
  - **Model Verification**: The **kinematic model** and **simulation service** are validated against real-world data, with **confidence bounds** (upper/lower limits) visualized in the dashboard.
  - **Uncertainty Handling**: Sensor noise (e.g., swing angle encoder) and environmental factors (e.g., wind speed) are accounted for in trajectory planning and validation.
  - **Validation Methods**: Reuses existing **model validation techniques** from [5], adapted for real-time digital twin validation.
  - **Fault Detection**: Time-series classification ([6]) identifies anomalies in sensor data or trajectory deviations, improving diagnostic accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The technical connection between the physical and digital twin is established via:
  - **Local Network Deployment**: All components (physical crane, digital twin services) are deployed on a **single personal computer**, minimizing latency.
  - **MQTT Protocol**: Used for lightweight, real-time communication between services (e.g., Logging Application → Database, Continuous Validation Service → Dashboard).
  - **Python-Based Interfaces**: The **Physical Crane Interface** connects directly to the **TMC4671-Eval motor controllers**, bypassing the Arduino for control commands. The Arduino is limited to sensor data acquisition and electromagnet control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The DT is hosted and deployed as follows:
  - **Local Deployment**: All services run on a **personal computer**, with the **Physical Crane** connected via USB/serial to the Arduino.
  - **Containerization**: **Database (TimescaleDB)**, **MQTT Broker**, and **Dashboard (Grafana)** are containerized using **Docker**, managed via a **docker-compose** file for easy setup.
  - **Python Module**: Core DT services (**gantrylib**) are packaged as a **pip-installable** Python module, enabling easy integration into other projects.
  - **Hardware**: The **Arduino** runs custom firmware (KiCad shield for sensor connectivity) and interfaces with the **PSU**, **motors**, and **sensors**.
  - **Documentation**: Hosted on **GitHub Pages** (MkDocs) with **README** and **BOM** (Bill-of-Materials) for reproducibility.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The DT provides the following insights and decision-making support:
  - **Trajectory Optimization**: Identifies swing-free paths to improve efficiency and reduce operational errors.
  - **Fault Localization**: Time-series classification ([6]) pinpoints anomalies (e.g., sensor failures, mechanical issues) for maintenance decisions.
  - **Operational Alerts**: **Continuous Validation Service** triggers alerts when trajectory deviations exceed thresholds, enabling proactive interventions.
  - **Historical Analysis**: **Grafana dashboards** visualize trends (e.g., wear-and-tear, environmental impacts) to inform long-term decisions.
  - **Research Insights**: Used to validate **continuous validation methods** ([4], [5]) and **DarTwin notation** ([7]) for digital twin evolution.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
Horizontal integration is limited to:
  - **Teaching Extensions**: The gantry crane was extended to model a **harbor system** (BIP 2024-2025), including container identification and ship digital twins with buoyancy models. These extensions are not yet fully integrated into the core exemplar.
  - **Future Framework Integration**: The exemplar could be adapted to use **digital twin frameworks** like **Eclipse Ditto** or **Eclipse BaSyx**, though the current implementation is ad-hoc and relies on open-source tools (e.g., MQTT, Docker). No external APIs (e.g., weather services) are integrated; wind speed is measured locally via an **anemometer**.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The DT adheres to the following standards and practices:
  - **Open-Source**: All software is released under the **MIT License**, ensuring reproducibility and extensibility.
  - **Interoperability**: Uses **open standards** like **MQTT**, **PostgreSQL (TimescaleDB)**, and **Docker** for compatibility.
  - **Modeling**: Leverages **C4 Model** for architecture visualization and **Capella (Arcadia)** for MBSE in teaching contexts.
  - **Hardware**: Custom **KiCad** designs for Arduino shields ensure modularity and reproducibility.
  - **No Proprietary Frameworks**: Avoids commercial digital twin platforms, opting for ad-hoc open-source solutions for flexibility.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
Security and safety considerations are implicitly addressed through:
  - **Local Deployment**: All components run on a **single personal computer**, reducing exposure to remote attacks.
  - **Real-Time Monitoring**: The **Continuous Validation Service** and **fault detection** (via time-series classification) help prevent unsafe operations by identifying deviations from expected behavior.
  - **Manual Operator Oversight**: The **Human Machine Interface** requires operator interaction for critical actions (e.g., homing, zeroing), ensuring human-in-the-loop safety.
  - **No Remote Execution**: The digital twin does not autonomously execute operations on the physical crane without operator confirmation, mitigating risks of unintended actions. However, explicit safety protocols (e.g., emergency stops, fail-safes) are not detailed in the document.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar Joost Mertens _Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_ _University of Antwerp_ Antwerpen, Belgium joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be _**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a** **large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly** **available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and** **reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-** **scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises** **both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical** **side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital** **side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,** **and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,** **data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup** **as use case in several previous publications where its functionality** **was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other** **freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope** **it can be used for future research or education on the topic of** **digital twins.** _**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-** **physical-system** I. I NTRODUCTION The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual systems and services derived therefrom. During operation data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3]. Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating research results requires relevant use cases. Such research results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the following digital twin related research: continuous validation methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a notation for system evolution [7]. In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in various domains would help reduce development overhead, and additionally also also help support the generalizability of research results. Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education is another field for which well described exemplars can be Joachim Denil _Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_ _University of Antwerp_ Antwerpen, Belgium joachim.denil@uantwerpen.be Fig. 1. The physical gantry crane. beneficial, aiding teachers with explaining and demonstrating the digital twin concept, and training engineers with the necessary experience to set up such systems in the real world. In this paper, we present an openly available digital twin exemplar of a lab-scale gantry crane. It is inspired by cranes in the harbor of Antwerp, which move containers between moored ships and the quay. It is a typical Cyber-Physical System and consists of: _•_ The physical gantry crane including its controller. _•_ The crane’s digital twin containing CAD models, services for data logging, data visualization, optimal trajectory generation, and continuous validation. The exemplar is hosted on Cosys-Lab’s organizational GitHub [1] . A picture of the physical crane is shown in fig. 1. The remainder of this paper is structured as follows: section II first discusses related exemplars from literature. Section III discusses our exemplar in detail, both architecturally and implementation wise. Afterwards section IV discusses the results and the publications that feature this exemplar. Next, section V discusses some limitations and future extensions. Finally section VI concludes the paper. 1 [https://github.com/Cosys-Lab/lab-scale-gantry-crane](https://github.com/Cosys-Lab/lab-scale-gantry-crane) II. B ACKGROUND & R ELATED W ORK A search with the query “digital twin exemplar” on Semantic Scholar, Google Scholar and GitHub’s search function yielded other openly available digital twin exemplars with accompanying publications. In what follows, we briefly discuss the most noteworthy ones. _A. ARCHES PiCar-X_ The PiCar-X is a little Raspberry-Pi based robotic car that is used by Barbie and Hasselbring as a demonstrator of various digital twin concepts [8], [9]. These digital twin concepts [10] were originally used in the ARCHES project on autonomous robotic networks [11], but the authors wanted to make these results reproducible in a lab-scale setting with an affordable and straightforward example. The exemplar is hosted on GitHub [2] and contains documentation on how to get the exemplar up and running. Emulators are also provided such that the physical system is not necessarily needed. The exemplar features technologies such as Docker containerization to encapsulate the examples and is built on the open ARCHES Digital Twin Framework which is built on top of the ROS (Robot Operation System) middleware. _B. GreenhouseDT_ GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse [12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with prerecorded data. _C. Air Quality Management_ The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14]. Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented digital twin pipeline. _D. Discussion_ The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses 2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X) 3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT) 4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar) Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system. a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are more ad-hoc. In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework. The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars. III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves small pucks which represents containers from one location to another. In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and describe the architecture of the system with a context and container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system. _A. Architecture_ Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital twin research on it. Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to keep the diagram readable. The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital twin if needed, consists of five containers. **Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane. 5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/) 6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for something that must be there for the system to work. Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system. **Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane. **Control Application:** a Python application that uses the crane interface to control the crane. **Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database. **Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the operator to configure and control the crane. The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe them bottom up. **Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data created by the system and its various services. **Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from the database. **Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no residual swinging motion at the end. **Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator **Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics between them and compares the value with an acceptable threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action. **MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between services. Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give an overview of the components powering it. We should note that for this physical system, we take a liberal interpretation of what goes in a C4 Model component diagram. Figure 4 shows this component diagram, again for brevity and readability only the most necessary relationships are shown. It contains the **Crane Frame** consisting of all the hardware constituting the physical structure of the crane. Two **Motors** and associated **Controllers**, one to control the cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement. An **Electromagnet** at the end of the hoist to pick up containers, a **Swing Angle Encoder** measures the swinging motion of the hoist, and an **Anemometer** measures the wind speed. An **Arduino** measures and controls those last three components. Lastly a **PSU** powers the electronics of the crane. _B. Implementation_ Regarding the implementation, we wanted to keep the operation of the system as simple as possible, that is, plug in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of the software on that computer. Figure 5 shows the distribution of the C4 Containers visually. For the implementation, we bundled all the Python C4 Containers in a Python module named _gantrylib_ . To use this module, it suffices to install it with _pip_ . This way it remains easy for researchers to implement additional digital twin services. For ease of deployment, the **Database**, **MQTT Broker** and **Dashboard** C4 Containers are containerized with Docker. A docker compose file is provided to bring the containers up and down with a single command. For the first time setup, all of the database and dashboard initialization are also automatically handled by this docker compose file. Furthermore some of the components warrant a small explanation for their implementation choices. For the database, the choice for TimescaleDB was driven by three factors: (i) it’s based on PostgreSQL, allowing for commonly known SQL queries for access. (ii) it’s optimized for time series data, which is what is logged. (iii) it’s open source. For the dashboard, the choice for Grafana was made because of (i) it’s wide support for data sources and (ii) again the fact that it’s open source. Lastly, for the MQTT Broker, the ubiquitous and open-source Eclipse Mosquitto was used. _C. Repository Organization_ Listing 1 shows the repository organization. The **BOM** folder contains the Bill-of-Materials. The **C4-diagrams** folder contains the diagrams used in this paper. The **CAD** folder contains CAD models of the crane made in Autodesk Fu sion360. The **arcadia** folder contains Arcadia models made in Capella for a lecture on Model-Based Systems Engineering that starred the crane. The **arduino** folder contains the code to deploy on the measurement and control Arduino, as well as hardware schematics and a board for a custom shield made in KiCad that simplifies connectivity of the sensor to the Arduino. The **docker** folder contains the docker compose file and all associated first-time setup files for the dashboard, database and MQTT broker. The **docs** folder contains the documentation made in MkDocs and hosted on GitHub Pages. The **examples** folder contains basic examples of the gantrylib module. The **gantrylib** folder then contains the module itself. The **test** folder contains test associated with the gantrylib module. The **LICENSE** file contains the MIT license under which the exemplar is published. The **README** gives a brief overview of the repository and redirects to github pages for the installation. Lastly, **mkdocs.yml** contains some MkDocs configuration and **setup.py** contains the metadata of the gantrylib module. **Listing 1** Repository organization. ./ |-- BOM/ |-- C4-diagrams |-- CAD/ |-- arcadia/ |-- arduino/ |-- docker/ |-- docs/ |-- examples/ |-- gantrylib/ |-- tests/ |-- LICENSE |-- README.md |-- mkdocs.yml |-- setup.py IV. R ESULTS Figures 6 to 8 show some results or output of this exemplar. Figure 6 shows the basic but functional Human Machine |Cart Motor Controller
[Component: TMC4671-Eval]
Motor controller of the cart motor|Control/|Cart Motor
[Component: Stepper]
Motor for cart movement| |---|---|---| |**Cart Motor Controller**
[Component: TMC4671-Eval]
Motor controller of the cart motor|**Monitor**|**Monitor**| |Hoist Motor Controller
[Component: TMC4671-Eval]
Motor controller of the hoist motor|Control/|Hoist Motor
[Component: Stepper]
Motor for hoist movement| |---|---|---| |**Hoist Motor Controller**
[Component: TMC4671-Eval]
Motor controller of the hoist motor|**Monitor**|**Monitor**| Fig. 4. Component Diagram of the Physical Crane. Fig. 5. Deployment of components on a personal computer. Fig. 6. The Human Machine Interface. Fig. 7. Docker compose startup. Interface the operator can use to control the crane. In it are controls for the crane’s movement, homing and zeroing, the real-time status and configurations of the logger w.r.t database writeout. Figure 7 shows that with the single docker compose command the containers and the associated network are up and running. Lastly, fig. 8 shows some traces on the dashboard. Those traces have also been simulated by the simulator, as indicated by the upper (in blue) and lower (in yellow) confidence bounds derived from the simulation. Fig. 8. Grafana Dashboard showing trajectory traces. _A. Usage in Publications_ The first publication using the exemplar is [4], a short abstract on the concept of continuous validation. While the paper itself did not yet feature the gantry crane, the accompanying poster used the crane as illustrative example. Later, in [5], we further explored the concept of continuous validation through the reuse of existing model validation techniques. Here the initial version of the crane exemplar was developed and used in the evaluation of the paper. For more detailled results w.r.t. the continuous validation digital twin we refer the reader to this publication. The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. On the one hand the development process of the crane was used to derive those evolutions, on the other hand we envisioned multiple future evolutions of the crane system to demonstrate the DarTwin notation. The latest use of the exemplar was in [6], where we explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques to find faults in systems in operation. In essence, we extended the continuous validation method of [5] with classification techniques to better pinpoint system faults in the exemplar. _B. Usage in Teaching_ At the University of Antwerp, we organized a Blended Intensive Programme (BIP) on the topic of Model Based Systems Engineering for Digital Twin Systems. Here the exemplar was used as project to work on. We expanded it to an entire harbor system, with a container identification system to intake containers in the harbor, and a ship digital twin with a buoyancy model to teach about correct container loading order. This BIP was organized during the first semester of academic year 2024-2025. In academic year 2025-2026 it will be revisited in the second semester. At the Gran Sasso Science Institute (GSSI) the exemplar was used during the SpaceRaise doctoral school to teach about digital twinning in practice [7] . On this first day talks were given on the topic of twinning in Systems Engineering, and the exemplar was used to practically summarize the topics presented that day. Specifically for this doctoral school, the Capella models were developed. V. D ISCUSSION AND F UTURE W ORK The gantry crane has proven it’s merit for both research and education, but nonetheless it has some limitations or shortcomings which we discuss here. _A. Cost_ Firstly, with the current BOM the crane is not low cost, at approximately 1250 Euro for one crane in parts. Almost half of this cost stems from the Trinamic Motor Drivers, for which a development kit is used. The logical next step is to forego the development kits and the measurement Arduino 7 [https://spaceraise.academy/#courses Week 2, May 19th 2025](https://spaceraise.academy/#courses) with its shield, and develop a custom driver board integrating these three microcontrollers. This would greatly bring the cost down. _B. Size_ The current size of the crane was chosen based on the dimensions of the location in the lab. This size, however, is larger than the common size of checked luggage accepted by airlines, making it cumbersome to transport. _C. Teaching Extensions_ During teaching we made extensions to the gantry crane: for the BIP more of the harbor was included, and for the doctoral school we focused on Capella models. We deemed those extensions a little too immature to include as features in this paper, in the future they must be further developed, especially the ship and harbor simulations for the BIP. _D. Lack of Physical Simulation_ Currently, there is no physical crane simulator. To make the exemplar even more accessible, a completely simulated version of the physical crane could be included, as was done in the exemplars mentioned in the related work. This researchers or educators can test the project without committing to building the rather costly crane. This could also open up uses on the topic of virtual commissioning. _E. Digital Twin Frameworks_ Over the past years a multitude of digital twin frameworks have been developed, many of them open-source [15], [16]. Examples are Eclipse Ditto, for Internet-of-Things devices and their digital twins, or Eclipse BaSyx for Asset Administration Shell driven digital twins. Our approach is much more ad-hoc, and more in line with the approach taken in [17]. We make use of open-source technologies, but none of them are tailored specifically to digital twins. One could argue for or against this, in our opinion the ad-hoc approach allows more freedom and less overhead, but usage of the frameworks would surely make the exemplar more industry relevant. VI. C ONCLUSION We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed to support digital twin research and education. It expands the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case, reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility was illustrated with examples from our personal academic publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar, it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which we provided pointers for future work. [2] C. Cimino, E. Negri, and L. Fumagalli, “Review of digital twin applications in manufacturing,” _Computers in Industry_, vol. 113, p. 103130, Dec. 2019. [3] O. C. Madubuike, C. J. Anumba, and R. [5] J. Mertens and J. Denil, “Reusing model validation methods for the continuous validation of digital twins of cyber-physical systems,” _Software_ _and Systems Modeling_, p. 23, Oct. 2024. [6] J. Mertens and J. Denil, “Localizing faults in digital twin models by using time series classification techniques,” in _2024 Winter Simulation_ _Conference (WSC)_, pp. 2868–2879, IEEE, Dec. 2024. [7] J.
